content.problemStatement:
Nitika was once reading a history book and wanted to analyze it. So she asked her brother to create a list of names of the various famous personalities in the book. Her brother gave Nitika the list. Nitika was furious when she saw the list. The names of the people were not properly formatted. She doesn't like this and would like to properly format it.

A name can have at most three parts: first name, middle name and last name. It will have at least one part. The last name is always present. The rules of formatting a name are very simple:



  **Only** the first letter of each part of the name should be capital.
  All the parts of the name except the last part should be represented by only two characters. The first character should be the first letter of the part and should be capitalized. The second character should be ".".


Let us look at some examples of formatting according to these rules:


gandhi -> Gandhi
mahatma gandhI -> M. Gandhi 
Mohndas KaramChand ganDhi -> M. K. Gandhi
constraints: Each part length is between 2 and 10 inclusive
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a name. A name consists of one to three space‑separated parts (first, optional middle, and last). Each part contains only English letters (a‑z, A‑Z).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output the formatted name on its own line. All parts except the last are represented by their capital initial followed by a dot. The last part is printed with only its first letter capitalised and the rest in lower case.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Each line is split into its parts. Single part "gandhi" becomes "Gandhi". Two parts produce an initial for the first part and the capitalised last name. Three parts produce initials for the first two parts followed by the formatted last name.

solution.approach: For each test case split the line by whitespace to obtain the parts. Let n be the number of parts. For i from 0 to n-2 output the capitalised first character of parts[i] followed by a dot and a space. Then output the last part with its first character upper‑cased and the remaining characters lower‑cased. Print a newline after each test case. The algorithm runs in O(total number of characters) time and O(1) extra space besides the input line.
solution.timeComplexity: O(L) where L is the total length of all input strings
solution.spaceComplexity: O(L) for storing the input lines (or O(1) auxiliary space per test case)

code.Python3:
import sys

def capitalize(s):
    return s[0].upper() + s[1:].lower() if s else ''

def solve():
    data=sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
    if not data: return
    T=int(data[0].strip())
    out=[]
    for i in range(1, T+1):
        parts=data[i].strip().split()
        formatted=[]
        for p in parts[:-1]:
            formatted.append(p[0].upper()+'.')
        formatted.append(capitalize(parts[-1]))
        out.append(' '.join(formatted))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__=='__main__':
    solve()
